questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes the purpose of an IP address?

choices.text: To encrypt data before it is transmitted across the internet
choices.explanation: Encryption is handled by protocols like HTTPS/TLS, not IP addresses. An IP address is a unique numeric identifier used to route data to the correct device.

choices.text: To uniquely identify a device on a network so that data can be routed to the correct destination
choices.explanation: Every device on a network has an IP address. Routers use these addresses to forward packets to their correct destination, much like a mailing address for digital data.

choices.text: To compress data packets so they travel faster across the network
choices.explanation: Compression reduces data size — it is not a function of IP addresses. IP addresses identify devices and enable routing of packets.

choices.text: To synchronize the clocks of all devices connected to the internet
choices.explanation: Clock synchronization is handled by protocols like NTP (Network Time Protocol). IP addresses serve as unique device identifiers for routing purposes.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes the role of a protocol in computer networking?

choices.text: A physical cable that connects computers in a network
choices.explanation: Physical cables are hardware — they are part of the network infrastructure, not protocols. A protocol is a set of agreed-upon rules for communication.

choices.text: An agreed-upon set of rules that govern how data is formatted and transmitted between devices
choices.explanation: A protocol defines the rules devices follow to communicate — how data is packaged, addressed, transmitted, and interpreted. Without common protocols, devices from different manufacturers could not communicate.

choices.text: A device that routes packets from one network to another
choices.explanation: This describes a router. A protocol is a set of communication rules, not a physical device.

choices.text: A storage medium used to back up data on a network
choices.explanation: Backup storage is a data management concept. A protocol is a standardized communication ruleset used by network devices.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes what a packet is in computer networking?

choices.text: A physical box used to transport hard drives between data centers
choices.explanation: In networking, a packet is a unit of digital data — not a physical container. It is transmitted electronically across a network.

choices.text: A small unit of data containing a portion of a message along with source and destination addresses
choices.explanation: A packet is a fragment of a larger message. It includes the data payload plus header information — including source and destination IP addresses — allowing it to be routed independently across a network.

choices.text: A type of encryption key used to secure data in transit
choices.explanation: An encryption key is a cryptographic value used to secure data. A packet is a unit of network data that may or may not be encrypted.

choices.text: A complete message transmitted in a single uninterrupted stream from sender to receiver
choices.explanation: Messages are broken into multiple packets for transmission — each packet travels independently and may take different routes. They are reassembled at the destination.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes bandwidth?

choices.text: The maximum amount of data that can be transmitted over a network connection in a given time period
choices.explanation: Bandwidth is the capacity of a network connection — typically measured in bits per second (bps). Higher bandwidth means more data can be transmitted per second.

choices.text: The time it takes for a single packet to travel from sender to receiver
choices.explanation: Travel time for a single packet is called latency or ping, not bandwidth. Bandwidth measures capacity — how much data can flow, not how fast one packet travels.

choices.text: The number of packets that are lost during transmission
choices.explanation: Lost packets are called packet loss. Bandwidth refers to the maximum data throughput capacity of a connection.

choices.text: The physical width of the cables used in a network
choices.explanation: Bandwidth is a measure of data capacity, not a physical dimension. Thicker cables may support higher bandwidth, but bandwidth itself is measured in data transfer rate (bits per second).

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: HTTP is a protocol used to transfer data on the web. Which of the following BEST describes what HTTP does?

choices.text: It encrypts all data transmitted between a web browser and a server
choices.explanation: HTTP does not encrypt data — HTTPS (HTTP with TLS) provides encryption. Standard HTTP transmits data in plaintext.

choices.text: It provides rules for how web browsers request web pages and how servers respond with that content
choices.explanation: HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) defines how clients (browsers) request resources from web servers, and how servers respond with those resources — it is the protocol underlying the World Wide Web.

choices.text: It assigns unique IP addresses to devices when they connect to the internet
choices.explanation: IP address assignment is handled by DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol), not HTTP. HTTP is specifically for web resource transfer.

choices.text: It compresses web page data to reduce bandwidth usage
choices.explanation: HTTP can optionally carry compressed content, but compression is not its primary function. HTTP defines the request-response communication rules for web content transfer.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A user types www.example.com into their browser. Before the browser can fetch the page, it asks a specialized server to translate that human-readable name into the numeric address (e.g., 93.184.216.34) that routers use to deliver the request. Once the browser has the numeric address, it can contact the correct server.

questions.question: The translation service described in the passage is best described as which of the following?

choices.text: DNS (Domain Name System)
choices.explanation: DNS is the internet's name-to-address translation service. When a browser needs to contact a site like example.com, it queries DNS to obtain the server's numeric IP address — exactly the lookup described in the passage.

choices.text: DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol)
choices.explanation: DHCP assigns IP addresses to devices when they first join a network — it does not translate domain names. The scenario describes looking up an existing address for a named site, which is a DNS operation.

choices.text: NAT (Network Address Translation)
choices.explanation: NAT rewrites addresses on outgoing packets so that many devices can share one public IP — it does not convert human-readable names to numeric addresses. The passage describes name resolution, which is DNS.

choices.text: HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol)
choices.explanation: HTTP is the protocol the browser uses to request the page after it already has the server's address. The passage specifically describes the earlier step of converting a name into an address, which is DNS.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A user sends a large video file to a friend over the internet. The file is broken into hundreds of smaller units, each labeled with the sender's address, the recipient's address, and the portion of the file it contains. Each unit is sent independently and may travel a different path across the internet, arriving at the destination in a different order. The receiving computer reassembles them into the original video file.

questions.question: Which of the following terms BEST describes each small unit described in the scenario?

choices.text: Protocol
choices.explanation: A protocol is a set of communication rules — not a unit of data. Each small unit of the transmitted file is called a packet.

choices.text: Packet
choices.explanation: Packets are the small units into which large messages are broken for transmission. Each packet contains payload data plus source/destination addressing information, and packets may travel independently across the network.

choices.text: Router
choices.explanation: A router is a network device that forwards packets — it is not the data unit itself. The small units of the video are packets.

choices.text: Bandwidth
choices.explanation: Bandwidth is the data capacity of a network connection, not a unit of data. Each small unit described is a packet.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A cloud storage app uploads a 50 MB file from a laptop to a remote server. The app splits the file into many smaller chunks, numbers them, and sends them across the network. As each chunk arrives, the server sends back a confirmation. If the sender does not receive a confirmation for a particular chunk, it resends that chunk. Once every chunk has been confirmed, the server reassembles the file in order.

questions.question: The protocol that provides the numbered, confirmed, and retransmitted delivery behavior described in the passage is best described as which of the following?

choices.text: TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)
choices.explanation: TCP is the reliable transport protocol that numbers chunks, waits for acknowledgements, retransmits lost chunks, and delivers data in order — exactly the behavior described in the passage.

choices.text: UDP (User Datagram Protocol)
choices.explanation: UDP is a connectionless protocol that sends packets without confirmations or retransmissions. The passage explicitly describes confirmations and retransmission of lost chunks, which is TCP behavior, not UDP.

choices.text: HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol)
choices.explanation: HTTP defines the format of web requests and responses. The chunking, acknowledgement, and retransmission described in the passage happen at a lower layer — TCP — even when HTTP is the application using it.

choices.text: DNS (Domain Name System)
choices.explanation: DNS translates domain names into numeric IP addresses. It does not handle splitting files into chunks or retransmitting lost data, which is the reliable-delivery job that TCP performs.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A network connecting two cities is built so that there are three different physical cable paths between them. When a construction crew accidentally cuts one cable, traffic between the two cities continues without interruption because data is automatically rerouted over the remaining two paths.

questions.question: The design property described in the passage is best referred to as which of the following?

choices.text: Redundancy
choices.explanation: Redundancy means providing multiple routing paths (or duplicate resources) so that if one path fails, data can still reach its destination via an alternative. The three-cable design and automatic rerouting after a cut is the defining example.

choices.text: Bandwidth
choices.explanation: Bandwidth is the amount of data a connection can carry per unit of time. The passage is about having alternative paths when one fails, not about the capacity of any single path.

choices.text: Latency
choices.explanation: Latency is the delay between sending and receiving data. It is not what allows the network to survive a cut cable — surviving a failure via alternate routes is redundancy.

choices.text: Encryption
choices.explanation: Encryption protects the confidentiality of data in transit — it does nothing to keep data flowing when a cable is cut. The ability to reroute around a failure is provided by redundancy.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A streaming service runs its website on a cluster of several identical servers. During a busy evening, two of the servers crash unexpectedly. Viewers notice no interruption — the remaining servers take over the workload and continue serving video without anyone needing to intervene.

questions.question: The property of the streaming service described in the passage is best referred to as which of the following?

choices.text: Fault tolerance
choices.explanation: Fault tolerance is the ability of a system to continue operating correctly when some of its components fail. The cluster surviving two crashed servers without user-visible disruption is the defining example.

choices.text: Abstraction
choices.explanation: Abstraction is a design technique that hides implementation details behind simpler interfaces. It does not describe keeping a system running when components fail, which is fault tolerance.

choices.text: Open access
choices.explanation: Open access refers to making information freely available to users. It is unrelated to a system's ability to survive component failures, which is fault tolerance.

choices.text: Compression
choices.explanation: Compression reduces data size to save storage or bandwidth. It has nothing to do with continuing to operate when servers crash, which is fault tolerance.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A student uses her laptop's internet connection to read interlinked articles in her browser, send a message through an email app, and play a multiplayer game. Only the articles — which are hosted as HTML documents linked to one another by URLs and loaded over HTTP in her browser — belong to one specific service running on top of that connection. The email app and the game use the same underlying connection but different services.

questions.question: The service that hosts the interlinked articles the student reads in her browser is best referred to as which of the following?

choices.text: The World Wide Web
choices.explanation: The World Wide Web is the service of interlinked HTML documents accessed via URLs over HTTP. The articles the student reads in her browser are part of the Web, which runs on top of the underlying internet.

choices.text: The internet itself
choices.explanation: The internet is the global network infrastructure that carries data for many services (email, games, the Web). The passage explicitly distinguishes the article-reading service from the shared underlying connection, so the answer is the Web, not the internet.

choices.text: A local area network (LAN)
choices.explanation: A LAN connects devices in a small geographic area like a home or office — it is not the service of interlinked web pages. The articles accessed over HTTP through a browser belong to the World Wide Web.

choices.text: The Domain Name System (DNS)
choices.explanation: DNS is the lookup service that translates domain names to IP addresses. It is not the collection of interlinked documents the student is reading — that collection is the World Wide Web.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A network diagram shows four computers connected in a ring: A → B → C → D → A. If the connection between B and C fails, computers A and D can no longer reach B or C.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes a limitation of this network design?

choices.text: The network has no redundancy — if any single connection fails, some computers become unreachable
choices.explanation: In a ring topology with no alternative paths, a single connection failure creates a break that isolates parts of the network. True fault tolerance requires multiple independent paths between nodes.

choices.text: The network has too many connections, which reduces bandwidth
choices.explanation: A ring with 4 nodes has only 4 connections — not too many. The limitation is insufficient connections (no redundant paths), not too many.

choices.text: The network cannot use TCP because there are no routers
choices.explanation: TCP can operate in any network — it does not require routers specifically. The limitation here is the single-path ring topology's lack of fault tolerance.

choices.text: The network uses too many IP addresses
choices.explanation: A 4-node network uses 4 IP addresses — this is not excessive. The design limitation is the lack of redundant routing paths.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A company sends a 10 MB file from an office in New York to a server in London. The file is broken into packets, and each packet independently travels across the internet — some taking a route through Toronto, others through Madrid, and some through both. All packets eventually arrive at the London server and are reassembled.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST explains why packets can take different routes?

choices.text: The internet uses packet switching, where each packet is routed independently based on network conditions
choices.explanation: Packet switching allows each packet to be routed independently based on current network conditions (congestion, available links). This improves efficiency and provides fault tolerance — if one route is congested or broken, packets can take alternative paths.

choices.text: The file was split into different formats — some packets use TCP and others use HTTP
choices.explanation: Packets in a single transmission use the same protocol. The reason for different routes is packet switching — each router independently decides the best next hop based on current conditions.

choices.text: Different packets have different bandwidth allocations, which determines their routing
choices.explanation: Individual packets do not have different bandwidth allocations. Routing decisions are made by routers based on network topology, congestion, and available paths.

choices.text: The internet requires all packets from a single file to travel on different routes to increase security
choices.explanation: Packets take different routes based on network conditions and routing algorithms — not as a security requirement. Security is provided by encryption, not path diversity.

questions.subject: ap-csp

rows: Each device connects to a single shared cable in a linear chain
All devices connect to a single central hub — if the hub fails, all devices lose connectivity
Each device has two independent connections to the network — if one fails, the other remains active
Devices are connected in a linear chain — removing any device breaks the chain

questions.question: Which design BEST demonstrates fault tolerance?

choices.text: Design A
choices.explanation: A linear chain (bus topology) has single points of failure — if any connection breaks, devices downstream are disconnected. This design offers no redundancy.

choices.text: Design B
choices.explanation: Design B has a single point of failure — if the hub fails, all devices lose connectivity. This is the opposite of fault tolerance.

choices.text: Design C
choices.explanation: Design C explicitly provides redundancy — each device has two independent connections. If one connection fails, the other keeps the device connected. This is fault tolerance: continued operation despite component failure.

choices.text: Design D
choices.explanation: A linear chain has no redundancy — removing one device or link breaks the entire chain. This design is highly vulnerable to failures.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes the difference between parallel computing and distributed computing?

choices.text: Parallel computing runs multiple tasks on multiple processors in the same computer; distributed computing divides a task across multiple separate computers
choices.explanation: Parallel computing uses multiple processors within one system. Distributed computing splits tasks across multiple independent computers connected by a network — enabling problems too large for any single machine.

choices.text: Parallel computing is only used for sorting algorithms; distributed computing is only used for web servers
choices.explanation: Both parallel and distributed computing have broad applications beyond specific algorithm types. The distinction is about hardware architecture — single multi-processor system vs. multiple networked computers.

choices.text: Parallel computing requires more bandwidth than distributed computing
choices.explanation: Parallel computing happens within a single machine and does not require network bandwidth. Distributed computing uses network connections — but bandwidth is not the defining distinction.

choices.text: Parallel and distributed computing are identical — both run tasks simultaneously on multiple processors
choices.explanation: While both involve simultaneous processing, they differ structurally: parallel = multiple processors in one machine; distributed = task split across multiple separate, networked computers.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A programmer uses 4 processors to run Task D. Which of the following BEST explains why the parallel time is 50 seconds rather than 25 seconds (which would be 100 seconds ÷ 4)?

choices.text: Task D uses a less efficient algorithm than the other tasks
choices.explanation: Algorithm efficiency affects sequential time, not the gap between expected and actual parallel speedup. The reason parallel time is not 1/4 of sequential time is that not all of Task D can be parallelized.

choices.text: Some parts of Task D must be executed sequentially and cannot be parallelized, limiting the achievable speedup
choices.explanation: The maximum speedup from parallelism is limited by the sequential (non-parallelizable) portion of the task. If some steps must happen in order, they cannot be split across processors — so the sequential portion acts as a ceiling on achievable speedup.

choices.text: Using 4 processors always produces exactly a 2× speedup, regardless of the task
choices.explanation: Speedup depends on how much of the task is parallelizable. Tasks A and B achieve 4× speedup; Task D achieves only 2× speedup because it has a larger sequential portion.

choices.text: The 4 processors are using different bandwidth allocations, which slows some of them down
choices.explanation: Bandwidth affects data transfer, not processor computation speed. The limitation here is that part of Task D must be executed sequentially, preventing full 4× parallelization.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A web browser needs to load a web page hosted at the URL https://www.example.com/page. The user's computer does not know the server's IP address. The browser must resolve the domain name to an IP address before connecting.

questions.question: Which network service does the browser use to resolve 'www.example.com' into an IP address?

choices.text: HTTP
choices.explanation: HTTP is the protocol used to request and transfer web content after the connection is established. It does not resolve domain names.

choices.text: TCP
choices.explanation: TCP provides reliable packet delivery — it ensures data arrives correctly. DNS resolves domain names to IP addresses.

choices.text: DNS
choices.explanation: DNS (Domain Name System) translates human-readable domain names like 'www.example.com' into numeric IP addresses that routers can use to direct network traffic to the correct server.

choices.text: Redundancy
choices.explanation: Redundancy is a network design property (multiple paths), not a service that resolves domain names. DNS performs the name-to-IP resolution.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A company is designing a network for a hospital. Patient records must remain accessible even if some network components fail. An IT consultant recommends a 'mesh' design where each node has multiple connections to other nodes, rather than a star design where all nodes connect to a single central switch.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST justifies the consultant's recommendation?

choices.text: The mesh design requires fewer IP addresses than the star design
choices.explanation: IP address count depends on the number of devices, not the topology. The justification for the mesh design is fault tolerance, not IP address efficiency.

choices.text: The mesh design provides redundant paths — if any single node or link fails, data can be rerouted, keeping records accessible
choices.explanation: In a hospital, continuous availability is critical. A mesh design with multiple paths is fault tolerant — any single failure can be routed around. The star design has a single point of failure (the central switch).

choices.text: The mesh design compresses patient records more efficiently than the star design
choices.explanation: Network topology does not affect data compression. The recommendation is justified by the mesh design's fault tolerance through redundant paths.

choices.text: The mesh design transmits data faster because it uses parallel computing
choices.explanation: Mesh topology provides routing redundancy — it does not inherently use parallel computing. The benefit is fault tolerance, not speed improvement.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: If a programmer runs this task using parallel computing (where segments 2, 3, and 4 run simultaneously), what is the minimum total execution time?

choices.text: 10 seconds
choices.explanation: 10 seconds is just the time for Segment 1. Segments 2-4 run in parallel (taking the max time: 8 seconds) and then Segment 5 takes 4 more seconds: 10 + 8 + 4 = 22 seconds total.

choices.text: 22 seconds
choices.explanation: Segment 1 (sequential): 10s. Segments 2, 3, 4 (parallel): the parallel group takes as long as the slowest = 8s (Segment 3). Segment 5 (sequential after parallel): 4s. Total = 10 + 8 + 4 = 22 seconds.

choices.text: 33 seconds
choices.explanation: 33 seconds would be the sum of all segments run sequentially (10+5+8+6+4=33). With parallel computing, Segments 2, 3, and 4 run simultaneously: 10 + max(5,8,6) + 4 = 10 + 8 + 4 = 22 seconds.

choices.text: 14 seconds
choices.explanation: 14 would equal Segment 1 + Segment 5 alone (10 + 4). The parallel segments must also complete before Segment 5 can run: 10 + 8 + 4 = 22 seconds.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A user visits a website that uses HTTP (not HTTPS). She uses the site to order food for delivery and types in her home address and credit card number.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes a security risk in this scenario?

choices.text: The website cannot store data because HTTP does not support databases
choices.explanation: HTTP has no restrictions on what a server stores in its database. The security risk is that HTTP transmits data in plaintext, which can be intercepted.

choices.text: HTTP transmits data in plaintext, so anyone who intercepts the packets can read the credit card number and home address
choices.explanation: HTTP is unencrypted — data is transmitted in readable plaintext. A network attacker (e.g., on a shared Wi-Fi network) could intercept the packets and read the sensitive information.

choices.text: The website uses too much bandwidth because HTTP is less efficient than HTTPS
choices.explanation: Bandwidth efficiency is not the concern here. The risk is that HTTP transmits sensitive data without encryption, making it readable if intercepted.

choices.text: The DNS server will expose the user's home address to third parties
choices.explanation: DNS resolves domain names to IP addresses — it does not transmit or see application-layer data like addresses or credit card numbers.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes scalability in the context of a computer system?

choices.text: The ability of a system to encrypt data as traffic increases
choices.explanation: Encryption is a security property. Scalability is about a system's ability to handle increased workload without performance degradation.

choices.text: The ability of a system to handle an increasing number of users or requests without a significant drop in performance
choices.explanation: Scalability means a system can grow to accommodate more users, data, or load while maintaining acceptable performance — a critical property of web services and distributed systems.

choices.text: The ability of a system to repair itself when hardware components fail
choices.explanation: Self-repair is related to fault tolerance, not scalability. Scalability addresses growth in load, not recovery from failures.

choices.text: The maximum bandwidth of a network connection
choices.explanation: Bandwidth is a capacity measurement for a specific link. Scalability refers to the system's ability to grow and handle increased demand.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: An internet service provider (ISP) builds a network connecting 10 cities. The network is designed so that every city has at least 3 independent connections to other cities. The ISP claims the network is highly fault tolerant.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST explains why the network design supports fault tolerance?

choices.text: With 3 independent connections per city, up to 2 connections to any single city can fail simultaneously without isolating that city
choices.explanation: If each city has at least 3 independent paths, losing 1 or 2 still leaves at least 1 active path. The city remains reachable as long as at least one connection is active — this is the essence of fault tolerance via redundancy.

choices.text: The network uses TCP, which automatically repairs broken connections
choices.explanation: TCP retransmits lost packets but cannot repair broken physical connections. Fault tolerance here comes from multiple independent network paths, not TCP's retransmission mechanism.

choices.text: Each city has a separate IP address, preventing routing conflicts when links fail
choices.explanation: Each city having unique IP addresses is a basic networking requirement, not a fault tolerance mechanism. Fault tolerance comes from the redundant multiple connections per city.

choices.text: The 10-city network is small enough that all packets arrive within 1 millisecond
choices.explanation: Speed is not fault tolerance. Fault tolerance is about continued operation when connections fail — the redundant connections (3 per city) provide this property.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A programmer runs this workflow on 3 processors, with chunk A, B, and C running simultaneously. What is the speedup compared to running sequentially on 1 processor?

choices.text: The speedup is 3× because 3 processors are used
choices.explanation: The speedup is not simply equal to the number of processors because the sequential portions (load + combine = 7s) cannot be parallelized. Sequential total = 37s; parallel = 17s. Speedup ≈ 2.18×, not 3×.

choices.text: The speedup is approximately 2.18× (sequential = 37s, parallel = 17s)
choices.explanation: Sequential: 4+10+10+10+3 = 37s. Parallel: 4s (load) + 10s (A, B, C run simultaneously — max = 10s) + 3s (combine) = 17s. Speedup = 37 ÷ 17 ≈ 2.18×.

choices.text: The speedup is approximately 1.3× because only the processing chunks are parallelized
choices.explanation: The parallel time is 17s and sequential is 37s. 37 ÷ 17 ≈ 2.18, not 1.3. The processing chunks alone reduce total time significantly.

choices.text: There is no speedup because the sequential portions prevent any parallel benefit
choices.explanation: The sequential portions (7 seconds total) do limit speedup, but the parallel chunks (30 seconds of work compressed into 10) provide a significant reduction from 37s to 17s — a real speedup.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A researcher is analyzing DNA sequences from 1 million genetic samples. Each analysis is completely independent — analyzing one sample does not affect or depend on any other. She has access to a computing cluster of 10,000 machines.

questions.question:
Select TWO answers.

Which of the following statements BEST describe this scenario?

choices.text: This task is well-suited for distributed computing because all 1 million samples can be processed independently across many machines
choices.explanation: When tasks are independent (embarrassingly parallel), they are ideal for distributed computing — different machines handle different samples simultaneously with no coordination overhead.

choices.text: The task cannot benefit from parallel processing because DNA analysis is undecidable
choices.explanation: DNA sequence analysis is decidable (algorithms exist). The independence of each analysis makes it ideal for parallel/distributed processing.

choices.text: Using 10,000 machines could reduce the total analysis time by approximately a factor of 10,000 since tasks are independent
choices.explanation: When tasks are fully independent, distributing across N machines reduces total time by approximately N. With 10,000 machines each handling 100 samples, the workload completes ~10,000× faster than sequential processing.

choices.text: Distributed computing is not appropriate because each machine needs the same IP address to share data
choices.explanation: Machines in a distributed system each have unique IP addresses — they communicate over a network. Shared IP addresses would cause routing failures, not enable communication.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A company runs a website that serves millions of users. The system architect needs to ensure the website remains available even if multiple servers fail simultaneously. The architect proposes placing servers in three different geographic data centers, with automatic failover — if one data center goes down, traffic is automatically routed to the remaining centers.

questions.question: Which of the following properties does this design BEST demonstrate, and why?

choices.text: Scalability, because multiple data centers can handle more users than a single center
choices.explanation: While multiple data centers do add capacity, the key property demonstrated here is fault tolerance — the automatic failover when a data center fails. Continued availability despite failures is fault tolerance, not just scalability.

choices.text: Fault tolerance, because the system continues operating even when entire data centers fail, due to geographic redundancy
choices.explanation: Geographic redundancy and automatic failover are fault tolerance mechanisms. If one data center fails, traffic automatically reroutes to the others — the service remains available. This is fault tolerance through redundancy.

choices.text: Bandwidth, because three data centers combined have three times the network bandwidth
choices.explanation: Bandwidth is a connection capacity measure. The design feature described — continued operation despite data center failure — is fault tolerance through geographic redundancy.

choices.text: Parallel computing, because each data center runs the same code simultaneously
choices.explanation: Parallel computing involves splitting a single task across multiple processors. Multiple data centers serving different users is load distribution — the key property enabling failure survival is fault tolerance.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
Select TWO answers.

A city is designing a smart traffic system that will send sensor data from thousands of intersections to a central server every second. Each sensor sends approximately 1 KB of data per second.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST describe challenges or considerations for this system?

choices.text: With thousands of sensors sending data every second, the system requires high bandwidth and scalable server infrastructure
choices.explanation: Thousands of sensors × 1 KB/sec = potentially gigabytes per second of data. This requires high-bandwidth network connections and servers that can scale to process and store this continuous high-volume data stream.

choices.text: The system should use DNS to compress sensor data before transmission
choices.explanation: DNS translates domain names to IP addresses — it does not compress data. Data compression (if used) would be handled by the application layer.

choices.text: Network redundancy is important because a failure that cuts off sensor data from key intersections could impair the city's ability to manage traffic
choices.explanation: If a single network failure disconnects multiple sensors, the central system loses real-time data needed for traffic management. Redundant paths ensure sensor data continues flowing even if individual network links fail.

choices.text: Each sensor must use TCP because packet switching is not available for city networks
choices.explanation: Packet switching is the fundamental mechanism of the internet and all modern IP networks. City networks use standard packet-switched infrastructure, and not all IoT sensors require TCP specifically.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A programmer is running a data analysis task. She tests the program on a single processor (taking 120 seconds) and with 6 processors (taking 30 seconds).

questions.question: Which of the following BEST explains why the program with 6 processors takes 30 seconds instead of 20 seconds (120 ÷ 6)?

choices.text: The program is undecidable, so additional processors do not help
choices.explanation: An undecidable program would not produce results with any number of processors. The program finishes in 30 seconds — it is decidable. The speedup limitation is due to sequential portions.

choices.text: Some portion of the program must run sequentially and cannot be divided across processors, limiting the maximum speedup
choices.explanation: When any portion of a program is sequential — meaning it cannot be parallelized — that portion takes the same time regardless of processor count. This creates a ceiling on speedup, pulling total time above the ideal 120÷6=20s.

choices.text: Adding more processors always reduces performance because they compete for bandwidth
choices.explanation: The program runs faster with 6 processors (30s vs 120s). More processors can improve performance — the limitation is the sequential portion of the program, not processor competition.

choices.text: The 6 processors each have lower clock speeds than the single processor
choices.explanation: Clock speed differences are not described in the scenario. The reason speedup is less than 6× is that some program steps must run sequentially and cannot be split across processors, regardless of how many processors are added.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A software company offers a web application that 10,000 users access simultaneously. They plan to grow to 1,000,000 users over the next two years. Their current infrastructure runs on a single powerful server.

questions.question:
Select TWO answers.

Which of the following BEST describe concerns with this architecture as the user base grows?

choices.text: A single server creates a single point of failure — if it goes down, all 1,000,000 users lose access
choices.explanation: A single server has no redundancy. Any failure — hardware, software, or network — takes down the entire service for all users. Fault tolerance requires distributing the service across multiple servers.

choices.text: A single server may not scale to handle 1,000,000 simultaneous users — distributing load across multiple servers would improve scalability
choices.explanation: A single server has finite processing capacity. As user load grows 100×, the server will likely become a bottleneck. Distributing load across multiple servers improves scalability.

choices.text: The server must change its IP address for each new user to ensure packets are routed correctly
choices.explanation: A server uses one (or a few) fixed IP addresses — it does not change addresses per user. Users' clients connect to the server's IP; the server handles multiple concurrent connections with the same IP.

choices.text: TCP must be replaced with HTTP to handle more than 100,000 simultaneous connections
choices.explanation: HTTP runs on top of TCP — replacing TCP with HTTP makes no sense. HTTP is the application protocol; TCP is the transport protocol. Neither needs to be replaced to scale — the architecture needs more servers.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
A network engineer designs two versions of a data center network:

Design X: Each server connects to one switch, and all switches connect to one central router. If the central router fails, no servers can communicate.

Design Y: Each server connects to two independent switches. Each switch connects to two independent routers. Multiple paths exist between any two servers.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST explains why Design Y is more appropriate for a production data center?

choices.text: Design Y uses fewer components, which reduces the probability of any single component failing
choices.explanation: Design Y uses MORE components (two switches, two routers per path). The benefit is not fewer components but more redundant paths that survive individual component failures.

choices.text: Design Y is fault tolerant — multiple independent paths mean any single component failure can be worked around, keeping all servers connected
choices.explanation: Design Y's redundant switches and routers mean no single point of failure. If one switch or router fails, packets reroute through the remaining devices — all servers stay connected.

choices.text: Design Y compresses data more efficiently because packets can choose shorter routes
choices.explanation: Path selection does not affect data compression. Design Y's advantage is fault tolerance through multiple redundant paths — not compression efficiency.

choices.text: Design Y is better because it uses DNS to resolve server addresses more quickly
choices.explanation: DNS performs name resolution — it is not affected by whether one or two routers are used. Design Y's advantage is fault tolerance through physical network redundancy.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
Select TWO answers.

A streaming video service uses a content delivery network (CDN) — copies of popular videos are stored on servers at hundreds of locations worldwide. When a user in Tokyo requests a video that originated in New York, the CDN serves it from a server in Tokyo rather than New York.

questions.question: Which of the following properties does the CDN design BEST demonstrate?

choices.text: Fault tolerance — if the New York server goes down, users can still access the video from Tokyo or other CDN nodes
choices.explanation: Geographic redundancy means the video is available from multiple locations. If the origin server fails, CDN nodes worldwide continue serving the content — this is fault tolerance through distributed redundancy.

choices.text: Scalability — serving content from local CDN nodes reduces load on the origin server and improves response times for large user populations
choices.explanation: CDNs distribute load away from a single origin server to many local servers. As user count grows globally, the CDN scales to serve users from nearby locations without overloading the origin.

choices.text: Packet switching — each video frame travels independently via a different CDN node
choices.explanation: Packet switching describes how individual packets are routed — it is not the same as distributing content across geographic CDN nodes. The CDN design demonstrates fault tolerance and scalability.

choices.text: Data abstraction — users do not need to know the IP addresses of CDN servers
choices.explanation: Users not knowing IP addresses is an abstraction layer, but the CDN design's primary properties demonstrated here are fault tolerance and scalability — not data abstraction.
